findings.description: No aggregate cap on liability was found.
findings.explanation: Absent a cap, liability is unbounded — an unusual posture for a commercial MSA.
findings.recommendation: Add an aggregate cap (commonly 12 months' fees) with explicit carve-outs.
findings.title: Aggregate liability cap missing

findings.description: Worker / party is identified as California-based but the contract selects Delaware law.
findings.explanation: Cal. Lab. Code § 925 voids out-of-state choice-of-law clauses in employment contracts entered into, modified, or extended after Jan. 1, 2017 — unless the employee was individually represented by counsel in negotiating that specific clause. Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600.5 (2024) extends similar reasoning to non-competes: they are unenforceable regardless of where signed if the worker is in California, and create a private right of action with attorneys' fees. This pattern is commonly used to evade California's strict non-compete prohibitions; courts (e.g., *Lyon v. Neustar*) routinely refuse to enforce it.
findings.recommendation: Either change governing law to California, or include a §925 counsel-representation acknowledgment. Note that §16600.5 may render any non-compete unenforceable even with §925-compliant choice-of-law language.
findings.title: Out-of-state choice-of-law on California-based party

findings.description: An IP assignment is present but pre-existing IP is not expressly carved out.

excerpt.text: 5. Intellectual Property Ownership. Customer retains all right, title, and interest in and to Customer's pre-existing data, materials, and intellectual property. All deliverables specifically created for Customer under a SOW shall be considered works made for hire to the maximum

findings.explanation: Without an explicit carve-out, a broad assignment can sweep in the assigning party's pre-existing IP. Modern drafting carves out 'Background IP' or 'Pre-existing IP' to scope the assignment.
findings.title: Pre-existing IP carve-out not stated

findings.description: No third-party IP infringement indemnification clause was found.
findings.explanation: A vendor-supplied product that infringes a third party's IP exposes the customer to suit. The standard allocation is that the vendor indemnifies for IP claims.
findings.recommendation: Add a third-party IP infringement indemnity (defense, settlement, damages) with the usual customer-cooperation conditions.
findings.title: IP infringement indemnity missing

findings.description: No indemnification covering breach of data protection, gross negligence, or wilful misconduct was found.
findings.explanation: These categories are unusually high-impact and routinely indemnified.
findings.recommendation: Add an indemnity for damages arising from gross negligence, wilful misconduct, or breach of data-protection obligations.
findings.title: Indemnity for data-protection / gross-negligence missing

findings.description: No background / foreground IP ownership clause was found.
findings.explanation: Without allocation, default copyright / patent rules govern, which rarely matches the parties' intent.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit allocation: each party retains its background IP; foreground IP ownership rule (usually customer for deliverables, vendor for tooling).
findings.title: Background / foreground IP allocation missing

findings.description: No compliance-with-laws or non-infringement warranty was found.
findings.explanation: Customers expect a vendor to warrant lawful operation and non-infringement.
findings.recommendation: Add: services and deliverables (a) comply with applicable laws and (b) do not infringe any third-party IP rights.
findings.title: Compliance-with-laws / non-infringement warranty missing

findings.description: No SLA / service level agreement / uptime commitment was found.
findings.explanation: An MSA for hosted services without an SLA leaves availability promises unenforceable.
findings.recommendation: Reference an SLA (attached or linked) with uptime, support response, and remedy schedules.
findings.title: SLA reference missing

findings.description: Used but not defined: Customer Data, Two Million Dollars.
findings.explanation: Title-Case phrases in a contract usually signal a defined term. When they aren't defined, readers must guess at the intended meaning. Either define them or use lowercase if the term is intended in its ordinary sense.
findings.recommendation: Add a definition for each genuinely defined term, or change the casing if the phrase is being used in its ordinary sense.
findings.title: Undefined Title-Case terms: 2

findings.description: The survival clause exists but does not name: IP ownership / assignment, indemnification.

excerpt.text: 6. Confidentiality. Each party (as "Receiving Party") shall hold in strict confidence all non-public information of the other party (as "Disclosing Party") designated as confidential or that reasonably should be understood to be confidential. Each party shall use such information

findings.explanation: Survival language is what keeps sticky obligations alive after a contract terminates. A survival clause that doesn't expressly enumerate the present-in-document confidentiality / IP / indemnity obligations creates ambiguity at the moment those obligations matter most — post-termination, when the contract has already ended.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit named references to the missing obligation(s): IP ownership / assignment, indemnification. Standard drafting names every sticky section by number or category in the survival clause.
findings.title: Survival clause does not name 2 sticky obligations

findings.description: Customer may terminate this Agreement without cause upon thirty (30) days prior written notice to Vendor. In the event of termination for convenience

excerpt.text: 10. Termination. (a) Termination for Cause. Either party may terminate this Agreement upon written notice if the other party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receiving written no

findings.explanation: An asymmetric termination-for-convenience right is sometimes intentional (e.g., paid-up vendors), but the asymmetry should be deliberate.
findings.title: Only one party may terminate for convenience

findings.description: The contract references data but does not separately allocate data ownership.

excerpt.text: 11. Data Portability and Return on Termination. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement for any reason, Vendor shall: (a) promptly provide Customer with a complete, machine-readable export of all Customer Data in a standard, non-pr

findings.explanation: IP and data ownership are different. For SaaS, the customer typically owns its data; the vendor typically owns usage analytics. Address each explicitly.
findings.title: Data ownership not separately addressed

findings.description: No indemnification covering breach of confidentiality was found.
findings.explanation: Confidentiality breaches often produce damages that are difficult to quantify; indemnification anchors the remedy.
findings.recommendation: Add an indemnity for damages arising from breach of confidentiality obligations.
findings.title: Confidentiality breach indemnity missing

findings.description: No termination-on-insolvency clause was found.
findings.explanation: 11 U.S.C. § 365 makes pure ipso-facto clauses unenforceable in some scenarios, but the contract should still anchor the parties' intent.
findings.recommendation: Add: termination on filing for bankruptcy, appointment of receiver, or assignment for the benefit of creditors.
findings.title: Termination for bankruptcy / insolvency missing

findings.description: No transition / wind-down / hosted-service continuation period was found.
findings.explanation: Without a wind-down, the customer can be cut off mid-migration.
findings.recommendation: Add: vendor will continue providing the service for up to N (e.g., 90) days post-termination at then-current rates to facilitate transition.
findings.title: Wind-down / transition assistance missing

findings.description: No bilateral force-majeure language was found.
findings.explanation: A one-sided force-majeure clause (vendor-only) is commercially abnormal; payment obligations are conventionally excluded.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'neither party shall be liable for delay or failure due to force majeure', with a payment-obligation carve-out.

source_citations.source: Commercial drafting baseline — balanced force majeure

findings.title: Balanced force majeure missing

findings.description: No amendment-in-writing or no-waiver boilerplate found.
findings.explanation: Without these, oral modifications and unintentional waivers become litigation risks.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'no amendment is effective unless in writing signed by both parties' and 'no failure to enforce shall be deemed a waiver.'
findings.title: Amendment-in-writing / no-waiver missing

findings.description: No clause was found addressing AI / generative AI usage by the vendor in service delivery.
findings.explanation: Increasingly the contract should disclose AI use, training data, and IP ownership of outputs (see the §49 AI Addendum playbook for the deep check).
findings.recommendation: Add a clause disclosing whether AI is used, naming material AI subprocessors, and addressing IP in outputs.
findings.title: AI usage clause missing

findings.description: Exclusive / limited remedy language is present but no UCC § 2-719(2) fail-of-essential-purpose escape was found.
findings.explanation: UCC § 2-719(2) preserves alternative remedies when a limited remedy fails of its essential purpose — anchoring this protects the customer from a remedy gap.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'if the limited remedy is found to fail of its essential purpose, the customer's other remedies under this Agreement and applicable law remain available.'
findings.title: UCC § 2-719 limited-remedy escape missing

findings.description: Only vendor bears this typically-mutual obligation.
findings.explanation: Obligations like confidentiality, indemnity, and representations are usually mutual. A one-sided version is sometimes intentional but worth confirming.
findings.title: Asymmetric representations obligation

findings.description: Each party's aggregate liability to the other party for all claims arising out of or related to this Agreement shall not | The aggregate liability cap in Section 8(a) shall not apply to: (i) Vendor's obligations under Section 7; (ii) either pa | This mutual exclusion of consequential damages shall not apply to breaches of confidentiality obligations.

excerpt.text: Each party's aggregate liability to the other party for all claims arising out of or related to this Agreement shall not exceed the greater of: (i) One Million Dollars ($1,000,000); or (ii) the total fees paid or payable by Customer to Vendor in the twelve (12) months immediately preceding the claim giving rise to liability.

findings.explanation: Negative covenants restrict what a party may do. Surfacing them collectively makes it easier to check they are intended and consistent with the overall deal.
findings.title: Negative covenants: 3

findings.description: incidental, special, exemplary, or consequential damages

excerpt.text: 8. Limitation of Liability. (a) Aggregate Liability Cap. Each party's aggregate liability to the other party for all claims arising out of or related to this Agreement shall not exceed the greater of: (i) One Million Dollars ($1,000,000); o

findings.explanation: A waiver of consequential, special, incidental, and punitive damages is standard in commercial contracts. The waiver should be mutual unless deliberately asymmetric.
findings.title: Consequential damages waiver present

findings.description: commercial general liability insurance with limits of at least Two Million Dollars ($2,000,000

excerpt.text: 9. Vendor Insurance. During the term of this Agreement and for two (2) years thereafter, Vendor shall maintain, at its own expense: (a) commercial general liability insurance with limits of at least Two Million Dollars ($2,000,000) per occurrence and Five Million Dollars ($5,000,000) in the aggregate; (b) professional

findings.explanation: Insurance levels should match the deal size and risk. Common minimums are $1M per occurrence CGL and $2M E&O for services contracts.
findings.title: Insurance requirements stated

findings.description: Indemnity clause appears to be missing: defense control, settlement consent.

excerpt.text: 7. Vendor Indemnification. Vendor shall defend and hold harmless Customer and its officers, directors, employees, and agents ("Customer Indemnitees") from and against any third-party claim, suit, or proceeding arising out of or relating to: (a) Vendor's breach of this Agreement,

findings.explanation: A complete indemnity clause specifies (a) the timeline and form for notice of a claim, (b) which party controls defense, and (c) whether settlement requires consent.
findings.title: Indemnity procedural elements missing: defense control, settlement consent

findings.description: The following defined terms are never used outside their definition: Customer Indemnitees.
findings.explanation: Defined terms that are not used downstream may be inherited from a template and obsolete. They clutter the contract and can confuse readers; either delete them or use them where intended.
findings.recommendation: Review each unused term and either delete it or wire it into the relevant clause.
findings.title: Defined terms with no uses: 1

findings.description: Provisions are stated to survive termination.

excerpt.text: 6. Confidentiality. Each party (as "Receiving Party") shall hold in strict confidence all non-public information of the other party (as "Disclosing Party") designated as confidential or that reasonably should be understood to be confidentia

findings.explanation: A survival clause names the obligations that outlast termination — typically confidentiality, indemnity, payment obligations accrued before termination, and choice of law.
findings.title: Survival clause present

findings.description: Survival clause does not appear to include: indemnity, payment, governing law.

excerpt.text: 6. Confidentiality. Each party (as "Receiving Party") shall hold in strict confidence all non-public information of the other party (as "Disclosing Party") designated as confidential or that reasonably should be understood to be confidentia

findings.explanation: Typical surviving obligations include confidentiality, indemnity, accrued payment obligations, and governing law. Missing any of these is common and worth confirming.
findings.title: Survival list may be missing categories: indemnity, payment, governing law
